findings.title: Citizens United After 10 Years: More Speech, Better Democracy
findings.summary: Institute for Free Speech argues that Citizens United v. FEC led to 'more speech, better democracy,' claiming there is 'no evidence' that corporate and union independent spending 'leads to corruption or the appearance of corruption' and that the decision strengthened rather than weakened democratic participation.
findings.whyBad: This piece commits a categorical falsehood by claiming 'no evidence' of corruption from post-Citizens United spending. Since 2010, academic research has documented the connection between campaign contributions and legislative outcomes (e.g., Ferguson et al., 2018 finding that congressional votes correlate with donor interests; Gilens & Page, 2014 finding that economic elites and organized business interests have substantial impact on policy while average citizens have near-zero). Dark money organizations have spent over $1 billion in federal elections since 2010, with massive single-donor contributions creating clear access and influence pathways (e.g., Sheldon Adelson's $150M+ across cycles correlated with policy outcomes on Israel and online gambling; the Koch network's $400M+ correlated with climate and tax policy outcomes). The 'more speech' framing ignores that the speech is dramatically unequal — a tiny number of wealthy donors and corporations dominate political spending. The rhetorical technique is bare assertion — stating 'no evidence' as fact while ignoring an entire academic literature to the contrary. The harm is providing intellectual cover for the campaign finance system that gives billionaires disproportionate influence over American democracy.

findings.title: Five Misconceptions About Dark Money
findings.summary: Institute for Free Speech argues that 'dark money' is 'merely a pejorative label for nonprofits' engaged in political speech, that disclosure requirements disproportionately harm grassroots organizations, and that compelled donor disclosure creates a chilling effect on First Amendment rights.
findings.whyBad: The piece reframes transparency as censorship — a technique of rhetorical inversion that takes a democratic accountability measure and rebrands it as an infringement on liberty. This ignores that the Supreme Court has repeatedly and explicitly upheld disclosure requirements as constitutional, including in the same Citizens United decision (2010) that struck down spending limits: 'disclosure permits citizens and shareholders to react to the speech of corporate entities in a proper way.' The 'chilling effect' argument fails to acknowledge that the same Court in NAACP v. Alabama protected disclosure only when it posed a demonstrable threat of harassment — not as a general principle against all disclosure. The 'grassroots burden' claim ignores that sophisticated dark money groups like those IFS defends have far more resources to comply with disclosure than small grassroots organizations. Since the 2010 Citizens United decision, dark money spending has topped $1 billion in federal elections (OpenSecrets), making the argument that disclosure is unnecessary or harmful a direct threat to democratic accountability. The real harm is providing legal and rhetorical cover for unlimited anonymous spending in elections.

findings.title: Voter Suppression? Dems Flat-Out Wrong, Census Data Gives Real Story
findings.summary: Von Spakovsky cites 2020 Census Bureau data showing Black turnout at 63% (up from 60% in 2016) and Asian American turnout at 59% (up from 49% in 2016) to argue that 'voter suppression' is a myth and critics are 'flat-out wrong.'
findings.whyBad: This argument uses selective data to imply that increased overall turnout proves no barriers exist — a classic cherry-picking technique that ignores three key facts. First, the 2020 election was anomalous: pandemic-era policies massively expanded mail voting and reduced in-person barriers, making it inappropriate for extrapolating to normal conditions. Second, aggregate turnout data cannot disprove that specific laws (voter ID, roll purges, reduced drop boxes) create barriers for specific individuals. Third, von Spakovsky's own Kansas case documented 31,000 citizens blocked from registering by a law he helped defend — citizens who never appeared in turnout data because they couldn't register at all. The piece ignores the distinction between being discouraged but voting anyway and being blocked entirely. The rhetorical technique is omission: presenting data that is technically true but irrelevant to the specific claim being rebutted. The harm is creating a misleading simplicity that allows policymakers to dismiss legitimate complaints of disenfranchisement.

findings.title: The Latest Election Data Show—Once Again—That 'Voter Suppression' Claim Is Just Propaganda
findings.summary: Hans von Spakovsky's April 2023 Heritage Foundation report arguing that 2022 election data from states like Georgia, Florida, and Texas proves that 'voter suppression' claims are 'just propaganda,' citing increased turnout in states that enacted restrictive voting laws.
findings.whyBad: This report commits the ecological fallacy — using aggregate turnout data to argue that no individual group was burdened, when in fact turnout increases can hide who was left out. The Heritage Foundation's own Election Fraud Database, which von Spakovsky manages, documents approximately 1,500 fraud cases over decades across hundreds of millions of votes cast — a rate of roughly 0.00006%, which directly undermines the premise that restrictive laws are needed. The Brennan Center found that the database 'substantially inflates and exaggerates the occurrence of voter fraud' and most cited cases 'would not be addressed by the voter suppression laws its staff supports.' The rhetorical technique is straw man: constructing a caricature of 'voter suppression' claims and labeling them 'propaganda' rather than engaging with the specific documented instances where ID laws blocked eligible voters. The harm is direct policy influence — Heritage Action used this frame to lobby for restrictive voting laws across battleground states.

findings.title: Hungary Prime Minister Viktor Orban on USAID, Trump, Immigration, NATO, and the Russia/Ukraine War
findings.summary: Carlson's February 2025 interview page introducing Hungarian Prime Minister Viktor Orbán as having 'been vindicated on pretty much everything' and featuring a 64-minute interview where Orbán claims Ukraine could become Europe's 'Afghanistan' and argues the war is about NATO enlargement, not about Ukraine.
findings.whyBad: Carlson provides an uncritical platform to an authoritarian leader who has dismantled independent media (Hungary ranked 85th in the 2024 Reporters Without Borders Press Freedom Index, down from 23rd in 2010), packed the courts, passed anti-LGBTQ+ legislation that the EU found violated fundamental rights, and implemented electoral changes that Freedom House describes as 'entrenching Fidesz's hold on power.' Carlson's framing of Orbán as 'vindicated on pretty much everything' normalizes democratic backsliding by presenting it as a successful governance model. His characterization that Hungary's press 'is much freer than ours' (from his 2021 broadcasts) is directly contradicted by every major press freedom index. This is not journalism interviewing a controversial figure — it is advocacy presenting an authoritarian model as aspirational to an American audience of millions. The harm is normalizing the idea that dismantling democratic institutions is acceptable if it produces desired cultural or policy outcomes.

findings.title: Kansas Republicans Are Letting Democrats Destroy Ballot Integrity Safeguards
findings.summary: Hans von Spakovsky argues that a 'meritless' Democratic lawsuit (League of Women Voters v. Schwab) seeks to 'eliminate the most basic safeguard in mail voting: verifying that the signature on a returned ballot matches that of the voter,' and warns that Kansas Republicans are failing to defend 'ballot integrity safeguards.'
findings.whyBad: Von Spakovsky manufactures a crisis narrative around signature verification for mail ballots — a safeguard that addresses a fraud problem that is virtually nonexistent. The same author was the key expert witness in Fish v. Kobach defending Kansas's proof-of-citizenship law, where a federal judge found his opinions were 'based on preconceived beliefs related to his advocacy' rather than evidence. That law blocked over 31,000 eligible citizens from registering. Von Spakovsky's own spreadsheet in that case identified only 38 alleged non-citizen registration cases in Kansas's second-largest county over two decades. His framing of routine mail ballot verification litigation as 'destroying ballot integrity' uses a slippery slope fallacy to justify restrictive policies with documented disenfranchisement effects. The real harm: this piece provides intellectual cover for rolling back mail voting access based on a nonexistent fraud crisis.

findings.title: We must pass voter ID — it is simply common sense
findings.summary: CA Post Editorial Board op-ed arguing voter ID is 'simply common sense' and 'standard practice in democracies all over the world,' dismissing disenfranchisement concerns by claiming 'turnout has tended to increase, not decrease, in places that have adopted voter ID' and that lawsuits have 'struggled to find any plaintiffs who have actually suffered the loss of their right to vote.'
findings.whyBad: The piece commits the rhetorical technique of appeal to common sense — framing a contested policy as obvious and beyond debate, thereby dismissing documented evidence of disproportionate disenfranchisement. Studies consistently find that strict voter ID laws disproportionately affect Black, Latino, elderly, and low-income voters who are less likely to possess qualifying ID (GAO, 2014; Brennan Center). The claim that no plaintiffs exist is false: courts in cases like Veasey v. Abbott (Texas) and NAACP v. McCrory (North Carolina) found that such laws imposed burdens on minority voters. The piece also ignores that millions of Americans lack government-issued photo ID — an estimated 11% of eligible voters according to a 2012 GAO study. The real-world harm is that this framing normalizes restrictive voting policies that studies show reduce turnout among already-marginalized populations.

findings.title: The depopulation agenda: How vaccines, AI and globalist elites are targeting humanity
findings.summary: The article argues that globalist elites backed by the WHO and WEF are deliberately reducing the world population using COVID-19 mRNA vaccines, AI-driven censorship, and social destabilization. It frames routine vaccination during pregnancy as turning women into 'pin cushions' receiving 'up to eight vaccine doses' and alleges the 'transgender movement pushes irreversible sterilization on children' as part of the same depopulation scheme.
findings.whyBad: The article claims COVID-19 mRNA vaccines have caused 'an estimated 100+ million deaths worldwide' citing Denis Rancourt, a number that dwarfs the entire confirmed global COVID death toll (~7 million) and has no basis in any peer-reviewed mortality study. It cherry-picks unverified VAERS reports as if they were proven causal injuries while omitting the CDC/EMA consensus that mRNA vaccines are safe and have saved millions of lives. The article weaves multiple unrelated grievances (vaccines, transgender healthcare, AI) into a single depopulation conspiracy narrative using a slippery slope fallacy to suggest any public health measure is a step toward genocide. The real-world harm is promoting complete vaccine refusal, which directly increases susceptibility to preventable diseases and undermines trust in all public health institutions.

findings.title: Does the SNP really believe there are 24 genders?
findings.summary: Joan Smith argues that the Scottish government's publication of 24 gender categories for official documents is 'nonsense,' claiming there are 'only two sexes' and that collecting gender data is 'an exercise in appeasing narcissists.' The piece frames transgender and non-binary identities as a 'reality-denying cult' and an 'ideology which most sensible people regard as delusional.'
findings.whyBad: The article deploys a pathologizing frame by asserting that gender diversity is a 'delusional' 'cult' and that only 'two sexes' exist — a claim that ignores the well-documented medical and scientific consensus from the American Medical Association, the American Psychological Association, and the World Health Organization that gender identity is distinct from biological sex and that transgender identities are not disorders. The rhetorical technique is a straw-man + slippery slope: it caricatures a bureaucratic taxonomy of 24 categories (designed for data collection on a spectrum that includes standard male/female/trans categories) as absurd proliferation, then uses that caricature to dismiss the entire concept of transgender authenticity. This rhetoric directly harms transgender people by legitimizing discrimination: when an authority figure with institutional credentials (former Chair of the Mayor of London's Violence Against Women and Girls Board) frames trans identities as delusional, it provides cover for policies that exclude, misgender, and deny healthcare to transgender individuals.

findings.title: Why Billionaires Are Good for Us
findings.summary: Argues that billionaires are engines of prosperity whose fortunes benefit everyone through innovation and job creation. Uses circular reasoning that free-market wealth accumulation is inherently deserved and good for society.
findings.whyBad: Presents absolute ownership norms as a moral good while omitting any discussion of how billionaire wealth is accumulated: labor exploitation, regulatory capture, inherited advantage, monopoly rents, and tax avoidance. Ignores that the Forbes 400 holds $4.5T — enough to lift every American living in poverty above the poverty line multiple times over. The 'innovation' frame ignores that most transformative R&D is publicly funded (NIH, DARPA, DOE), not the result of billionaire risk-taking. This piece normalizes extreme wealth concentration as virtuous rather than a policy choice, making it harder to build political will for redistribution.

findings.title: The Welfare Trap: How Democrats Built a Nation of Lazy Dependents
findings.summary: Argues that 'healthy, able-bodied adults are abusing SNAP benefits' and that 'Democratic policies reward laziness and punish hard work,' portraying welfare recipients as lazy dependents rather than people in need of assistance.
findings.whyBad: Explicitly dehumanizes welfare recipients by labeling them 'lazy dependents,' frames poverty as a moral character defect, and weaponizes resentment to justify cuts to food assistance programs that millions rely on.

findings.title: Fast Food Workers: You Don't Deserve $15 an Hour to Flip Burgers — and That's OK
findings.summary: Argues that fast food workers do not deserve a living wage for their labor and that poverty-level wages are the deserved outcome of individual choices about what jobs to pursue.
findings.whyBad: Directly tells workers they deserve poverty wages, erases the dignity of essential service labor, shifts blame entirely onto workers for their own low pay while ignoring that employers profit from underpaying labor, and frames basic economic security as something that must be 'earned' through upward mobility rather than hard work.

findings.title: Poverty in America: What the Data Reveal
findings.summary: Argues that 'there is no child poverty without family poverty which is frequently the result of decisions by individual adults who have ignored their parental responsibilities' and claims 'almost 90 percent of poor children' live in households with certain family structure choices.
findings.whyBad: Falsely attributes child poverty almost entirely to parental 'irresponsibility' rather than structural factors like wage stagnation, housing costs, or lack of social support, providing a policy-justification framework for cutting assistance to poor families.

findings.title: New Report Shows More Americans Dependent on Welfare Checks at the Expense of Work
findings.summary: An official House committee report framing welfare receipt as creating a 'dependency' that 'diminishes the human spirit, and traps people in poverty,' pathologizing the act of receiving assistance while omitting the economic conditions that make it necessary.
findings.whyBad: Coming from a congressional committee with direct power over welfare policy, this official government document treats reliance on safety-net programs as a spiritual and moral failure rather than a rational response to economic hardship, directly informing legislative proposals to cut benefits.

findings.title: The human side of poverty: Why poor people make bad decisions
findings.summary: Frames poor people's behavior as a series of 'impulsive and bad decisions — eating junk food, smoking, having children with multiple partners' — deploying behavioral economics to explain poverty while omitting structural context like wages, costs, and systemic barriers.
findings.whyBad: Publishes a classic 'blaming the victim' narrative in a major newspaper that catalogues poor people's personal habits as the cause of their poverty, reinforcing stereotypes that poverty results from poor impulse control rather than material deprivation and lack of opportunity.

findings.title: Oscar Lewis, Personal Responsibility, and Poverty
findings.summary: Selectively invokes anthropologist Oscar Lewis's 'culture of poverty' concept to argue that personal responsibility, not structural factors, causes poverty, while ignoring that Lewis blamed the 'capitalist mode of production' for trapping people in poverty.
findings.whyBad: Deliberately misrepresents a scholar's work by quoting him selectively — citing his 'culture of poverty' observations while suppressing his central conclusion that capitalism is the root cause — in a transparent ideological maneuver to blame the poor for being poor.

findings.title: Poverty: Is it Circumstances or Decision-Making?
findings.summary: Argues that 'poverty in the US is heavily influenced by bad decision-making,' citing bankruptcy studies to claim personal behavior rather than systemic factors is the primary driver of poverty.
findings.whyBad: Presents a false dichotomy that ignores decades of research on structural determinants of poverty — wage theft, housing discrimination, medical debt, regressive taxation — and reduces a complex systemic problem to individual financial illiteracy and poor choices.

findings.title: Counterpoint: When it Comes to the Gig Economy, the Only Thing to Fear is Fear Itself
findings.summary: The R Street Institute argues that independent contractors 'overwhelmingly prefer their status as contractors' and that AB5's reclassification push is based on 'flawed assumptions,' advocating for portable benefits that preserve the contractor model and keep cost and risk on workers.
findings.whyBad: A think tank provides academic cover for misclassification by arguing that the absence of all employer-provided protections is actually what workers want, and proposes 'portable benefits' as a policy solution that would preserve the core exploitative structure while giving it a more palatable name.

findings.title: Working Together Priorities
findings.summary: Uber's official policy page frames preserving independent contractor status as essential to maintaining flexibility that workers 'value,' proposing only voluntary benefits while resisting any reclassification that would require healthcare, overtime, or workers' compensation.
findings.whyBad: Uber uses attractive language about flexibility to normalize a business model that systematically denies workers healthcare, overtime pay, workers' compensation, unemployment insurance, and paid sick leave, while transferring all vehicle expenses and business risk onto drivers.

findings.title: DoorDash Statement on DOL Final Rule on Worker Classification
findings.summary: DoorDash asserts that 'Dashers are properly classified as independent contractors under the FLSA' and that the company 'does not anticipate this rule will affect its business model,' accompanied by a 'freedom to dash' framing that presents total risk transfer to workers as entrepreneurial liberty.
findings.whyBad: DoorDash categorically denies any misclassification problem while running PR that rebrands the absence of minimum wage guarantees, paid sick days, overtime, and expense reimbursement as 'freedom,' weaponizing the word against workers' material interests.

findings.title: Why Billionaires Are Good for Us
findings.summary: Argues that billionaires are engines of prosperity whose fortunes benefit everyone through innovation, job creation, and philanthropy, using a tautological framework where any wealth accumulated in a free market is by definition deserved.
findings.whyBad: Uses circular reasoning (free market wealth equals deserved) to absolve extreme wealth of any systemic critique. Erases labor exploitation, regulatory capture, and government subsidies. Published by FEE, a Koch-funded think tank producing aggressive pro-billionaire propaganda.

findings.title: Do Billionaires Deserve Their Wealth? It Depends
findings.summary: Applies Ayn Rand moral philosophy to argue that billionaires who earned wealth through their own mind and effort deserve it absolutely, categorically rejecting systemic analysis of labor exploitation, market manipulation, or regulatory capture.
findings.whyBad: Provides philosophical absolutism that serves as ideological cover for policy-focused think tanks. Frames any critique of billionaire wealth as immoral. The Randian framework is unfalsifiable: any wealth in a free market is axiomatically deserved.

findings.title: Why Billionaires Should Exist
findings.summary: Argues that efforts to tax billionaires into extinction misunderstand their essential role as capital allocators and risk-takers whose wealth is necessary for economic innovation.
findings.whyBad: Frames billionaire wealth not merely as acceptable but as existentially necessary for society. Published by a Stanford-affiliated institution lending academic prestige to a position that protects extreme wealth concentration. Omits evidence that capital is allocated efficiently without billionaire intermediation.

findings.title: Proposed Patent Value Tax Threatens Innovation and Is Unconstitutional
findings.summary: Argues that taxing unrealized gains on intellectual property would harm the innovation sector and destroy incentives for patent creation.
findings.whyBad: Conflates taxing extreme unrealized wealth with taxing innovation itself. Ignores that most foundational patents emerge from publicly funded research (NIH, NSF, DARPA), not billionaire-funded efforts. Frames a modest wealth tax as existential threat without engaging counterevidence.

findings.title: The Great Enduring Myth: Stock Buybacks Hurt Workers
findings.summary: Claims the idea buybacks hurt workers is false because companies maintain separate budgets for wages and capital allocation, with wages determined by productivity and labor markets independent of buyback decisions.
findings.whyBad: Uses corporate accounting technicalities to deflect from real-world choices between returning cash to shareholders and investing in workers. Omits evidence that CEO compensation tied to EPS targets creates perverse buyback incentives, and that companies conducting massive buybacks have simultaneously laid off workers. Heritage is a Koch-funded institution producing consistent anti-labor policy.

findings.title: Well Done, Billionaires!
findings.summary: Celebrates billionaires as creators of value whose wealth is a moral good, framing any criticism as envy-driven class warfare.
findings.whyBad: Openly celebratory propaganda that frames moral judgment as settled: billionaires are good, critics are resentful. No engagement with any structural critique. The title itself signals the piece functions as cheerleading rather than analysis. FEE content is designed to inoculate readers against considering systemic inequality.

findings.title: Elizabeth Warren's Wealth Tax Is Punishment, Not Taxation
findings.summary: Frames wealth tax proposals as punitive and vindictive rather than legitimate policy, arguing they represent a dangerous expansion of government power that would ultimately target the middle class.
findings.whyBad: Employs slippery-slope rhetoric (wealth tax today leads to middle class tax tomorrow) without evidence. Frames progressive taxation as fundamentally illegitimate (punishment) rather than a policy choice. Part of a coordinated campaign by libertarian media to delegitimize any tax on extreme wealth.

findings.title: No, Teachers Are Not Underpaid
findings.summary: Argues that teacher compensation is fair when accounting for benefits, schedule, and job security, claiming the underpaid narrative is a union-perpetuated myth.
findings.whyBad: Actively undermines one of the most widely recognized labor justice issues in the US. Teacher undercompensation is well-documented with a wage penalty of approximately 20% versus similarly educated professionals. FEE selectively cherry-picks total compensation while ignoring chronic underfunding, burnout rates, and secondary jobs. Directly serves wealthy interests by suppressing sympathy for public sector workers.

findings.title: The Minimum Wage: Job Killer in Disguise
findings.summary: Argues that minimum wage increases destroy low-skilled jobs, hurt teenagers, and ultimately harm the very workers they aim to help. Uses CBO job-loss estimates outside their confidence intervals and cites discredited Seattle minimum wage studies.
findings.whyBad: Selectively uses the high-end of CBO job-loss estimates while ignoring the majority of peer-reviewed research (including the 2021 Congressional Minimum Wage study and meta-analyses by Dube, Cengiz, et al.) showing minimal to zero employment effects from moderate minimum wage increases. Frames poverty-level wages as a natural market outcome rather than a policy choice. Used by business lobbies to defeat every state and federal minimum wage increase since 2000.

findings.title: Why the U.S. Doesn't Have a Wealth Inequality Problem
findings.summary: Claims that standard wealth inequality measures are misleading because consumption inequality has not risen as dramatically as income or wealth concentration. Argues that focusing on the top 1% distracts from actual economic progress.
findings.whyBad: Relies on expired pre-2019 consumption survey data and ignores that top-decile wealth share doubled from 60% (1980) to 77% (2023) per Federal Reserve data. The 'consumption inequality' frame is a well-known rhetorical tactic that sidesteps the reality of asset concentration: the Forbes 400 now holds $4.5T, up from $1T in 2010. Used to delegitimize every policy proposal (wealth tax, estate tax, financial transaction tax) aimed at reducing concentrated wealth.

findings.title: Ten Thousand Commandments
findings.summary: Annual report that tallies the total estimated cost of federal regulations, presenting regulation as a hidden tax burden. Concludes that deregulation would unlock economic growth and that most regulations impose costs that outweigh their benefits.
findings.whyBad: Deliberately counts gross regulatory costs without weighing against benefits (cleaner air, safer workplaces, prevented financial crises). Does not distinguish between net-positive regulations (FDA drug safety, clean water rules) and genuinely burdensome ones. The 'hidden tax' framing is an ideological premise, not an empirical finding. Used as the primary deregulation bible by every administration since Reagan — cited in executive orders mandating two-for-one repeal requirements.

findings.title: An Overview of Capital Gains Taxes
findings.summary: Presents the case for lowering capital gains tax rates, arguing that lower rates encourage investment, boost productivity, and ultimately increase wages for workers. Frames capital gains taxation as 'double taxation' that discourages risk-taking.
findings.whyBad: Assumes a direct, proportional chain from capital gains tax cuts to worker wage gains — but post-2000 data shows a decoupling: productivity up ~70% while median wages rose ~18%. The 'double taxation' framing ignores that retained corporate earnings are never taxed at the individual level for most shareholders. No mention that capital gains tax preferences (step-up in basis at death, carried interest loophole, 1031 exchanges) already make effective rates on capital income far below the statutory rate. Used to defend proposals that 90%+ of benefits would flow to the top 10%.

findings.title: Biden's Tax Plan Would Crush Economic Growth
findings.summary: Heritage Foundation analysis arguing that Biden-era proposed tax increases (corporate rate, top marginal rate, capital gains) would destroy jobs, reduce GDP, and make the US uncompetitive. Uses Heritage's IGM dynamic scoring model to generate worst-case macroeconomic projections.
findings.whyBad: Relies on Heritage's IGM model which assumes extreme supply-side elasticities rejected by the CBO, JCT, Treasury OTA, and Penn Wharton Budget Model. The same model predicted TCJA would boost GDP by 3-4% permanently — actual post-2017 GDP growth was ~2.5% and has since reverted. No acknowledgment that corporate effective tax rates post-TCJA were already near historic lows (11% effective rate for S&P 500 in 2018). Used to oppose every component of Biden's Build Back Better agenda, preserving a tax code under which 55 Fortune 500 companies paid $0 in federal tax in 2020.

findings.title: The Laffer Curve: Past, Present, and Future
findings.summary: Argues that tax rate cuts can increase government revenue by incentivizing economic activity, presenting the Laffer Curve as an empirically validated law. Omits extensive CBO, Treasury, and Penn Wharton analyses showing TCJA dynamic feedback offset only 10-20% of static revenue loss, not the 100%+ needed for cuts to 'pay for themselves.'
findings.whyBad: Presents a contested theoretical curve as settled science; ignores the Kansas 2012 natural experiment where deep tax cuts collapsed revenue and triggered a fiscal crisis; selectively cites historical episodes (1980s rate cuts) while omitting that subsequent base broadening, not the rate cuts alone, stabilized revenue. Functions as the canonical reference work for 'tax cuts pay for themselves' claims that lawmakers cite to justify regressive tax policy.
